steps.title: Identify the grammatical structures
steps.content:
Left of blank: A dependent relative clause interrupting the main clause.
Right of blank: The main verb of the independent clause.

steps.title: Apply the punctuation rule
steps.content:
The relative clause "which takes place in a mid-twentieth-century rural Alaskan fishing village" interrupts the main clause between subject ("novel") and main verb ("tells"). A comma must close the interrupting clause so the main verb can resume.
  • Choice A — ‘village: tells’: "...rural Alaskan fishing village: tells the story" — a colon cannot close a relative clause; colons introduce lists or explanations, not resume interrupted main clauses. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘village. Tells’: "...rural Alaskan fishing village. Tells the story" — the period ends the sentence at "village"; "Tells" then becomes a verb with no subject — a fragment. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘village, tells’: "...rural Alaskan fishing village, tells the story" — the comma closes the relative clause and lets the main verb "tells" resume with its subject "novel." ✓
  • Choice D — ‘village tells’: "...rural Alaskan fishing village tells the story" — without a comma, "village tells" reads as if "village" is the subject of "tells," not the end of the relative clause. ✗
That confirms C is correct.
